technology.name: Mechanistic Novelty Detection System for Scientific Research
technology.description: A novelty detection system for scientific research, that achieves high-recall detection by operationalizing novelty detection mechanistically.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Idea Novelty Checker: Retrieval‑augmented LLM system for literature‑grounded novelty assessment
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper presents Idea Novelty Checker, an end‑to‑end pipeline that automatically judges the novelty of a scientific idea. It first retrieves a broad set of potentially relevant papers using keyword expansion, snippet search, and seed‑paper recommendation via the Semantic Scholar API. The candidate set is filtered with SPECTER‑2 embedding similarity and then re‑ranked by a facet‑aware LLM re‑ranker (RankGPT) that scores papers on purpose, mechanism, evaluation, and application facets. Finally, GPT‑4o is prompted with expert‑annotated in‑context examples to produce a binary novelty decision together with a literature‑grounded rationale. The implementation, prompts, and a curated expert‑labeled dataset are released as open‑source resources.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment of Scientific Ideas
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: End-to-end retrieval-augmented system specifically designed for assessing novelty of scientific ideas with facet-aware re-ranking and LLM-based judgment.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Facet‑based definition of scientific idea novelty
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The authors define novelty of a scientific idea as a deviation in at least one of three core facets—purpose (the problem addressed), mechanism (the technical approach), or evaluation (the validation method)—or as a novel combination or application of these facets. This definition is derived from a formative expert study and builds on prior work that decomposes research contributions into purpose, mechanism, and evaluation facets. The facet‑based definition serves both as a conceptual framework for assessing novelty and as an operational metric that guides retrieval, re‑ranking, and in‑context prompting within the Idea Novelty Checker system.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment of Scientific Ideas
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Mechanistic definition of scientific novelty via purpose, mechanism, and evaluation facets; directly operationalizes how novelty should be assessed.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Method for detecting scientific novelty via conceptual link recombination
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper introduces a scalable text‑based pipeline that (i) extracts salient scientific concepts from millions of dissertation abstracts using structural topic modeling (STM) and FREX scoring, (ii) identifies novel conceptual co‑occurrences (new links) by comparing each thesis to the accumulated concept network and applying a log‑odds significance filter, and (iii) quantifies a thesis's novelty as the count of such new links. This operationalization treats scientific innovation as the introduction of previously unseen concept pairings in the scholarly corpus and enables population‑scale measurement of novelty across three decades of US PhD dissertations.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: The Diversity–Innovation Paradox in Science
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Scalable mechanistic pipeline that operationalizes scientific novelty as novel concept co-occurrences in scholarly corpora using topic modeling.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Formative expert study on novelty evaluation challenges
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The authors performed a two‑phase expert study on 51 scientific ideas (46 generated by the Scideator system and 5 from real conference submissions) to assess novelty judgments. They measured inter‑annotator agreement with Cohen's Kappa (0.64 → 0.68) and identified two primary challenges: (i) the inherent subjectivity of novelty judgments and (ii) the difficulty of retrieving all relevant prior work when relying on keyword‑only retrieval. These findings motivated the design of a high‑quality retrieval pipeline and the facet‑based novelty definition used in the Idea Novelty Checker.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Literature-Grounded Novelty Assessment of Scientific Ideas
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Expert study identifying key challenges in scientific novelty assessment (subjectivity, retrieval difficulty) that motivate system design.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Analysis of mismatch between normal‑text and research‑paper novelty detection
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper empirically demonstrates that traditional normal‑text novelty detection methods (e.g., TF‑IDF weighting and co‑occurrence graph features) perform poorly when applied to scholarly articles, whereas features derived from entity‑based citation graphs (keyword and topic graphs) capture both the static and dynamic aspects of research‑paper novelty as defined in prior literature. Quantitative experiments on a large arXiv corpus and a small human annotation study support the claim that novelty in scholarly documents is fundamentally different from novelty in ordinary text. The analysis explains why entity‑based citation features are more effective for detecting novel research contributions.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Evaluating Research Novelty Detection: Counterfactual Approaches
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Demonstrates that research-paper novelty requires entity-citation features rather than standard text-novelty methods; essential for scientific specificity.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Four‑Facet Conceptual Framework for Document‑Level Novelty
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The authors introduce a structured conceptual framework that defines document‑level novelty through four inter‑related properties: Relevance (topic alignment with prior knowledge), Diversity (amount of new information), Relativity (novelty measured against a specific source set), and Temporality (chronological update). This framework formalizes how novelty should be reasoned about, guides the selection of source and target documents, informs the annotation guidelines for the TAP‑DLND 1.0 corpus, and underpins the feature engineering for the novelty detection classifier. By articulating these properties, the work provides a clear, reusable lens for future research on document redundancy and novelty detection.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: TAP-DLND 1.0 : A Corpus for Document Level Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Provides four-facet conceptual framework (Relevance, Diversity, Relativity, Temporality) that formalizes how novelty should be reasoned about.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Contextual focus‑based novelty detection system
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper introduces a sentence‑level novelty detection system for the TREC Novelty Track that (i) counts previously unseen terms weighted by lexical category (person, location, common noun, verb), (ii) maintains a focus variable indicating whether the preceding sentence was novel or old, and (iii) applies a cascade of learned thresholds that incorporate contextual cues such as pronoun‑initiated sentences and low‑content‑word sentences. Each sentence is processed by a named‑entity recognizer and a finite‑state parser to obtain category information, after which weighted novelty scores are compared to thresholds learned by a randomized hill‑climbing algorithm. The system achieves high precision while capturing bursts of novel information without exhaustive pairwise similarity computation.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Context and Learning in Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Mechanistic sentence-level novelty detector using term-weight thresholds, contextual cues, and learned cascades; emphasizes precision without exhaustive similarity.
Simple mechanistic cosine-similarity baseline with tuned threshold achieving high-recall novelty detection.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Annotation Guidelines and Protocol for Document-Level Novelty
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The authors devise a systematic annotation protocol for labeling news articles as novel or non-novel with respect to a set of source documents. The guidelines operationalize four novelty dimensions—Relevance, Diversity, Relativity, and Temporality—to decide whether a target document provides sufficient new information. Three postgraduate annotators independently apply the protocol, disagreements are resolved by majority voting, and inter-annotator reliability is quantified using Fleiss' Kappa (κ = 0.82), demonstrating high labeling consistency.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: TAP-DLND 1.0 : A Corpus for Document Level Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Systematic annotation protocol operationalizing novelty dimensions with high inter-annotator reliability (kappa=0.82); provides labeled benchmark data.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Formulation of Document-Level Novelty Detection as a Binary Classification Task
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper reconceptualises novelty detection at the document level by framing it as a two‑class supervised classification problem: given a set of previously seen (source) documents, an incoming target document is labelled novel or non‑novel based on the amount of new information it contains. This formulation extends the predominantly sentence‑level novelty work to full documents and explicitly incorporates the four criteria identified for novelty—relevance, diversity, relativity and temporality—into the task definition. By casting the problem as binary classification, the authors enable the use of modern machine‑learning models and standard evaluation protocols.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: *Novelty Goes Deep.* A Deep Neural Solution To Document Level Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Frames novelty detection as binary classification task explicitly incorporating relevance, diversity, relativity, and temporality criteria.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Mathematical Formalization and Threshold‑Independent Evaluation Protocol for NoveltyTask
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper rigorously defines the NoveltyTask pipeline by introducing formal notation for the initial training set (Dᵀ), the novelty‑detection evaluation set (EvalDet), the feedback set (Dᶠ) containing correctly flagged novel instances, and the novelty‑accommodation evaluation set (EvalAcc). Building on this formalism, it proposes a suite of evaluation metrics: micro‑F1 over all classes, class‑wise F1Known and F1Novel, and a threshold‑independent protocol that ranks instances by a confidence score, treats the least‑confident instances as reported novelties, and aggregates precision, recall and F1 across the full range using area‑under‑curve (AUC). This enables fair, comparable assessment of systems regardless of how many novelties they report and directly links detection quality to accommodation performance.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: A Unified Evaluation Framework for Novelty Detection and Accommodation in NLP with an Instantiation in Authorship Attribution
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Formal mathematical definition of NoveltyTask evaluation protocol with threshold-independent AUC-based metrics for fair system comparison.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Reformulation of document-level novelty detection as a multi-premise entailment (MPE) problem
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper reconceptualizes document-level novelty detection by treating the target document as a hypothesis and the set of source sentences as multiple premises. Non-novelty is defined as entailment of the hypothesis by the conjunction of the premises, enabling the use of natural language inference (NLI) models for the task. This formulation addresses the limitations of traditional information-retrieval based novelty detection and motivates multi-hop reasoning over several source texts.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Novelty Detection: A Perspective from Natural Language Processing
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Mechanistic reformulation of novelty detection as multi-premise entailment problem, enabling use of NLI models for judgment.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Formulation of Document-Level Novelty Detection as a Textual Entailment Task
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper reconceptualises novelty detection at the document level by casting it as a textual entailment (TE) problem. A target document is labelled non‑novel if it is entailed by any of the on‑topic source documents; otherwise it is labelled novel. This extends the sentence‑level TE‑based novelty sub‑tasks of RTE‑6 and RTE‑7 to full documents and provides a unified perspective that links lexical similarity, entailment decisions, and novelty assessment.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Document Level Novelty Detection : Textual Entailment Lends a Helping Hand
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Operationalizes document-level novelty mechanistically via textual entailment; links lexical similarity and novelty assessment.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: KL-divergence as a strong baseline feature for sentence-level novelty detection
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper demonstrates that computing the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the term-frequency distribution of a candidate sentence and the aggregated distribution of previously seen sentences provides a highly discriminative single feature for novelty detection. Using this feature alone in a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier yields an average F1 measure of 0.618 on the TREC 2004 novelty-track task 2, matching the performance of top-ranking systems that employed far more complex feature sets and linguistic processing.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Graph-Based Text Representation for Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Demonstrates KL-divergence as highly discriminative single mechanistic feature for novelty detection achieving competitive performance.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: IDF‑based document novelty scoring function
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper introduces a novelty detection algorithm that assigns a novelty score to each incoming document by aggregating the Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) values of its terms, optionally weighted by term frequency and normalized by document length. This document‑to‑summary scoring replaces exhaustive pairwise similarity calculations, achieving O(|d|) computational complexity independent of the size of the previously seen collection. By exploiting the historical IDF of the evolving corpus, rare terms are treated as indicators of novelty, enabling fast and memory‑efficient novelty detection in large, time‑evolving text streams.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Using temporal IDF for efficient novelty detection in text streams
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Mechanistic IDF-based novelty scoring function with O(d) complexity; operationalizes novelty as term rarity in evolving corpus.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: State-of-the-art online novelty detection system (All + Recursive + DNN) surpasses prior TREC 2004 best
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: By integrating the four lightweight sentence-level novelty features, recursive feature averaging, and a bottleneck deep neural network transformation, the authors achieve an F‑score of 0.625 on the TREC 2004 novelty track, a statistically significant improvement over the previous best system (F‑score 0.622). This demonstrates that the combined approach attains state‑of‑the‑art performance while preserving online O(W) scalability, where W is the number of unique words observed so far.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Online Sentence Novelty Scoring for Topical Document Streams
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Achieves state-of-the-art performance by integrating lightweight mechanistic features with recursive averaging and neural transformation.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: First Deep Neural Solution for Document-Level Novelty Detection without Hand-Crafted Features
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The authors present a deep neural architecture that classifies an incoming document as novel or non‑novel with respect to a set of previously seen source documents, thereby removing the need for manually engineered lexical or statistical features. The system builds a Relative Document Vector (RDV) by encoding each target sentence together with its most similar source sentence, concatenating their embeddings, absolute differences and element‑wise products, and feeds the resulting matrix into a convolutional neural network (RDV‑CNN) for end‑to‑end learning. This is claimed to be the first application of deep learning to the document‑level novelty detection task.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: *Novelty Goes Deep.* A Deep Neural Solution To Document Level Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: First deep neural end-to-end approach for document-level novelty using relative document vectors; removes need for hand-crafted features.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Comprehensive Empirical Comparison of Document-Level Novelty Detection Approaches
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper conducts a 10‑fold cross‑validation study on the TAP‑DLND 1.0 benchmark, evaluating a Random Forest classifier that uses hybrid semantic‑lexical features against several established baselines (Jaccard similarity + Logistic Regression, Set Difference, Cosine tf‑idf distance, Kullback‑Leibler divergence, IDF‑based novelty scoring, and an entropy‑based innovativeness scoring method). Performance is reported in terms of precision, recall, F1‑score and overall accuracy for both novel and non‑novel classes. The proposed system achieves the highest accuracy (79.2 %) and balanced precision/recall, demonstrating the effectiveness of the feature set and the Random Forest model for document‑level novelty detection.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: TAP-DLND 1.0 : A Corpus for Document Level Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Empirical comparison of novelty detection approaches on benchmark TAP-DLND corpus, demonstrating effectiveness of hybrid semantic-lexical features.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Novelty Characterization via Attention Weights
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The PAT‑SND system leverages the attention scores produced by its Relation‑Aware Property Attention Network (PAT) over the property‑value pairs of the two entities to generate human‑readable explanations for why a factual sentence is classified as novel. By ranking properties according to their attention weights, the model highlights the most influential attributes (e.g., occupation, description) that conflict with the predicted relation, providing automatic novelty characterization without additional supervision. The authors introduce a Novelty Characterization Score and demonstrate that the top‑1, top‑2 and top‑3 attended properties match human‑annotated key properties with high accuracy.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Semantic Novelty Detection and Characterization in Factual Text Involving Named Entities
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Mechanistic interpretable approach using attention weights over entity properties to characterize and explain novelty decisions.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: State-of-the-art semantic novelty detection performance on NFTD
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The PAT‑SND system achieves an AUC of 90.37 ± 0.5 on the NFTD test set, markedly outperforming ten baseline one‑class novelty detectors whose AUC scores range from 44.16 to 60.12. This result demonstrates that the proposed two‑stage architecture, together with its contrastive training regime and attention‑based reasoning over Wikidata background knowledge, sets a new performance ceiling for the newly defined semantic novelty detection task involving named entities.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Semantic Novelty Detection and Characterization in Factual Text Involving Named Entities
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: State-of-the-art semantic novelty detection system achieving high AUC via contrastive training and attention-based reasoning over background knowledge.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: NoveltyTask: Unified Multi‑Stage Task for Novelty Detection and Accommodation
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper introduces NoveltyTask, a unified problem formulation that evaluates a system's ability to (i) detect instances belonging to previously unseen classes (novelty detection) and (ii) incorporate correctly identified novel instances to improve classification over an expanded label set (novelty accommodation). The task is defined as a controlled, pipelined two‑stage process: an initial training phase on known classes, a detection phase where a (K+1)‑class classifier must either assign a known label or flag an instance as novel, a feedback phase that reveals ground‑truth labels for correctly flagged novelties, and an accommodation phase where the system must classify over (K+N) classes. This formulation makes the trade‑off between reporting novelties and classifying known instances explicit and enables systematic study of their interaction across any NLP task.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: A Unified Evaluation Framework for Novelty Detection and Accommodation in NLP with an Instantiation in Authorship Attribution
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Unified task formulation for novelty detection and accommodation with explicit two-stage pipeline making detection-classification trade-offs explicit.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Adaptation of Six Novelty‑Detection Algorithms for NLP
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper implements and adapts six established novelty‑detection techniques—Maximum Softmax Probability (MaxProb), Euclidean distance, Mahalanobis distance, Mean‑Complement (CompMean), the Placeholder algorithm, and Few‑Shot Open‑Set Recognition (Few‑Shot OSR)—to the NLP setting by applying them on top of a fine‑tuned BERT‑base classifier for the authorship‑attribution instantiation of NoveltyTask. For each method a confidence score is derived from the model’s outputs, enabling ranking of instances for novelty reporting.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: A Unified Evaluation Framework for Novelty Detection and Accommodation in NLP with an Instantiation in Authorship Attribution
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Implements and adapts six established novelty-detection algorithms (MaxProb, Mahalanobis, etc.) to NLP via fine-tuned BERT embeddings.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Empirical evaluation of TM-based novelty detection on five text datasets
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper conducts a systematic empirical study of the proposed Tsetlin Machine (TM) novelty‑detection pipeline on five publicly available text corpora (20‑Newsgroup, CMU Movie Summary, Spooky Author Identification, Web of Science, and BBC Sports). Using a TM with 5,000 clauses trained for 100 epochs, per‑class novelty scores are extracted and fed to downstream classifiers (decision tree, k‑nearest neighbour, SVM, logistic regression, and multilayer perceptron). The method is compared against seven established novelty‑detection baselines and achieves the best performance on three datasets and second or third best on the remaining two, demonstrating both effectiveness and robustness.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Measuring the Novelty of Natural Language Text Using the Conjunctive Clauses of a Tsetlin Machine Text Classifier
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Systematic empirical evaluation of Tsetlin Machine-based novelty detection on five text datasets, demonstrating robustness across domains.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Empirical Evaluation of Baseline Novelty‑Detection Algorithms on NoveltyTask
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper conducts a systematic empirical study of the six adapted novelty‑detection baselines on the authorship‑attribution instantiation of NoveltyTask, measuring detection performance across a range of reported novelties using micro‑F1, F1Known, F1Novel, and AUC metrics. Results show that MaxProb and Mahalanobis distance achieve the highest overall detection AUC, while all methods obtain low scores on novel classes, highlighting the difficulty of novelty detection in this setting.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: A Unified Evaluation Framework for Novelty Detection and Accommodation in NLP with an Instantiation in Authorship Attribution
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Empirical evaluation of baseline novelty-detection algorithms on NoveltyTask using micro-F1, per-class F1, and AUC metrics.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Empirical evaluation achieving top precision in TREC 2004 Novelty Track
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The authors submitted five system runs (Prec1, Prec2, Recall, Cosine, Combo) to Task 2 of the TREC 2004 Novelty Track, which requires sentence‑level novelty detection on a pre‑selected set of relevant sentences. Using the official TREC evaluation scripts they measured precision, recall, F‑measure and output length, showing that the precision‑oriented run Prec2 attained 0.61 precision—about 20 % higher than any other participant—while the Recall run tied for the highest F‑measure. These results demonstrate that the focus‑based novelty detector substantially improves precision without catastrophic loss of recall.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Context and Learning in Novelty Detection
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Empirical evaluation on TREC 2004 Novelty Track achieving top precision without catastrophic recall loss; validates focus-based approach.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Scholarly novelty assessment task formulation
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper defines a new evaluation task in which a system is given the titles, abstracts, and optional metadata of two scholarly papers and must decide which paper is more novel. Novelty is operationalized by assuming the later‑published paper in each pair is more novel, creating a binary classification problem specific to the scholarly domain. This formulation contrasts with existing creativity benchmarks that focus on semantic novelty and is intended to support scientific discovery workflows such as peer review and literature triage.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing Large Language Models for Novelty Assessment in Scholarly Publications
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Defines scholarly novelty assessment task via time-based comparison of paper pairs; provides evaluation task for research domain.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Counterfactual simulation framework for evaluating research paper novelty detection
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper proposes a novel evaluation framework for research paper novelty detection that replaces unavailable ground‑truth novelty labels with counterfactual simulations. Two orthogonal proxies are defined: (i) a time‑based proxy that asks models to distinguish papers from an earlier time t and a later time t + d, and (ii) an impact‑based proxy that uses future citation counts and test‑of‑time awards as signals of novelty. The framework operationalises these proxies as four concrete tasks—classification, ranking, correlation, and feature‑selection—providing a systematic, reproducible protocol for assessing novelty‑detection models without expert annotations.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Evaluating Research Novelty Detection: Counterfactual Approaches
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Proposes counterfactual evaluation framework using time and impact proxies when ground-truth labels unavailable; addresses evaluation challenge.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Ablation study of embedding components for novelty detection
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The authors conduct a controlled ablation experiment to assess how the two embedding components—local embedding (LE) and keyword‑guided discriminative embedding (DE)—affect the binary discrimination between known‑topic and novel‑topic documents. Four variants are evaluated: (i) both LE and DE enabled, (ii) only LE, (iii) only DE, and (iv) neither component. Precision, recall, and F1 scores are reported for each variant, demonstrating that the combination of LE and DE yields the highest novelty‑detection performance.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: TaxoCom: Topic Taxonomy Completion with Hierarchical Discovery of Novel Topic Clusters
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Ablation study demonstrating importance of combining local and discriminative embeddings for binary novel-topic classification.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Sentence-level novelty detection task formulation
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper formally defines a novel information-retrieval task in which a system must return sentences that are both on-topic (relevant) and contain information that has not appeared earlier in the ordered document stream (novel). It treats relevance as a passage‑retrieval problem and novelty as a filtering problem, requiring the system to maintain a memory of previously seen content. Sentences are chosen as the atomic retrieval unit to avoid the triviality of document‑level novelty and to better model a user who skims a stream of documents. The formulation includes explicit constraints such as chronological ordering and the requirement that system output be an unranked set of sentence identifiers.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: Novelty Detection: The TREC Experience
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Foundational sentence-level novelty task formulation treating novelty as filtering on relevant documents; basis for document-level extensions.

ranked_prerequisites.contribution_name: Incorporation of novelty‑detection and structured digital abstracts
ranked_prerequisites.contribution_description: The paper advocates adding capabilities to detect novel findings in articles and to leverage structured digital abstracts that encode experimental results in a machine‑readable format, thereby supporting curators in identifying new information.
ranked_prerequisites.paper_title: BioCreative III interactive task: an overview
ranked_prerequisites.explanation: Mentions novelty-detection incorporation with structured abstracts; minimal technical contribution.
End-to-end retrieval-augmented system specifically designed for assessing novelty of scientific ideas with facet-aware re-ranking and LLM-based judgment.

Expert study identifying key challenges in scientific novelty assessment (subjectivity, retrieval difficulty) that motivate system design.

Mechanistic sentence-level novelty detector using term-weight thresholds, contextual cues, and learned cascades; emphasizes precision without exhaustive similarity.
Simple mechanistic cosine-similarity baseline with tuned threshold achieving high-recall novelty detection.

Formal mathematical definition of NoveltyTask evaluation protocol with threshold-independent AUC-based metrics for fair system comparison.

Operationalizes document-level novelty mechanistically via textual entailment; links lexical similarity and novelty assessment.
Demonstrates KL-divergence as highly discriminative single mechanistic feature for novelty detection achieving competitive performance.
Mechanistic IDF-based novelty scoring function with O(d) complexity; operationalizes novelty as term rarity in evolving corpus.
Achieves state-of-the-art performance by integrating lightweight mechanistic features with recursive averaging and neural transformation.
First deep neural end-to-end approach for document-level novelty using relative document vectors; removes need for hand-crafted features.
Empirical comparison of novelty detection approaches on benchmark TAP-DLND corpus, demonstrating effectiveness of hybrid semantic-lexical features.
Mechanistic interpretable approach using attention weights over entity properties to characterize and explain novelty decisions.
State-of-the-art semantic novelty detection system achieving high AUC via contrastive training and attention-based reasoning over background knowledge.
Unified task formulation for novelty detection and accommodation with explicit two-stage pipeline making detection-classification trade-offs explicit.
Implements and adapts six established novelty-detection algorithms (MaxProb, Mahalanobis, etc.) to NLP via fine-tuned BERT embeddings.
Systematic empirical evaluation of Tsetlin Machine-based novelty detection on five text datasets, demonstrating robustness across domains.
Empirical evaluation of baseline novelty-detection algorithms on NoveltyTask using micro-F1, per-class F1, and AUC metrics.
Empirical evaluation on TREC 2004 Novelty Track achieving top precision without catastrophic recall loss; validates focus-based approach.
Defines scholarly novelty assessment task via time-based comparison of paper pairs; provides evaluation task for research domain.
Proposes counterfactual evaluation framework using time and impact proxies when ground-truth labels unavailable; addresses evaluation challenge.
Ablation study demonstrating importance of combining local and discriminative embeddings for binary novel-topic classification.
Foundational sentence-level novelty task formulation treating novelty as filtering on relevant documents; basis for document-level extensions.
Mentions novelty-detection incorporation with structured abstracts; minimal technical contribution.
